data.title: Year in rugby: South Africa dominate, France win Six Nations, England on the rise
data.description:
South Africa started and finished ‌2025 as the world’s No 1-ranked side, and their extraordinary depth and monstrous scrum suggest they will be the team to beat at the 2027 Rugby World Cup in Australia.
The Springboks retained the Rugby Championship title, inflicted a record test defeat on old foes New Zealand, completed comprehensive wins in France and Ireland, and racked up a record score against an ailing Wales side in Cardiff.
It was not a perfect 12 months, however, with a second-half capitulation at Ellis Park against ⁠Australia, who claimed a rare win in Johannesburg, and a poor performance in defeat by New Zealand as they failed to break an 88-year Auckland hoodoo.
But having used more than 50 players in 2025, South Africa managed to win 12 of their 14 tests, most of them easily, all the while bedding in a more expansive playing style under attack coach Tony Brown that secured a historic 43-10 win in Wellington over the All Blacks.
Sacha Feinberg-Mngomezulu emerged as the new star, ‍racking up the most tries ever by a Springbok fly-half with nine between September and November alone, surpassing Morne Steyn and Handre Pollard.

It was the ‍continued evolution of their scrum, however, no matter who made up the forward pack, that will sound alarm bells for rivals. When the Springboks needed a penalty, the set-piece almost always delivered.
Hooker Malcolm Marx won World Rugby Player of the ‌Year, though loose head prop Ox Nche was their most potent scrummager and but for an injury in the second half of the season might well have won ‍that award.
Challengers for next World Cup
The way the Springboks dismantled the Ireland scrum in Dublin was one of the most powerful performances ever seen in the Test arena.
With South Africa having tied coach Rassie Erasmus down to a new contract until 2031, plenty of international rivals will have sleepless nights on how to counter the Springboks’ power at the next two World Cups.
France won the 2025 Six Nations Championship ‌but had ⁠a mixed 12 months having sent a weakened team on a three-match tour of New Zealand that ended in a 3-0 defeat.
As Ireland also faltered, it was England who built up a head of steam under coach Steve Borthwick, missing out on the Six Nations title by a single point in a tightly contested championship.
They then won 11 tests in a row to climb from seventh to third in the world rankings and look the ‌most likely challengers to the Springboks in two years’ time.
Coach Andy Farrell’s British & Irish Lions completed a 2-1 series victory in Australia, a contest that was closer than many envisaged as the Wallabies blew hot and cold throughout the year.

Australia enter a new era under ‌coach Les Kiss in the middle of ‌2026 on shaky ground as they build towards the global finals on home soil.
England lifted the Women’s Rugby World Cup trophy with a comfortable 33-13 victory over Canada at a sold-out Twickenham. The tournament broke records for attendances and had more than a billion social-media impressions in a boost to the women’s game.
A new competition independent from World Rugby called Rugby360 was mooted throughout the year but came under fire from the establishment before its proposed start date was moved to 2028.

data.title: US politicians back Coupang, seethe over South Korea’s ‘campaign of aggression’
data.description:
Coupang’s significant spending on lobbying politicians in the United States has apparently paid off, with Republicans criticising the South Korean government’s ongoing investigation into the Seattle-headquartered e-commerce firm.
They have denounced South Korea’s investigation into Coupang as an attempt to favour China while remaining silent on the company’s massive data breach of the personal information of 33.7 million users, allegedly by a former employee who is a Chinese national.
Amid growing suspicion that Coupang has been trying to turn the matter into a diplomatic feud, Seoul is now keeping a close watch on the company’s suspected connections with US government officials and lawmakers.
Robert O’Brien, who served as national security adviser during the first Donald Trump administration, said on Tuesday that the National Assembly’s aggressive targeting of Coupang will set the stage for further discriminatory measures by the Korean Fair Trade Commission and broader regulatory barriers towards US firms.
“Trump has worked hard to rebalance the trade relationship with Korea, and it would be very unfortunate if Korea undermines his efforts by targeting US tech firms,” he wrote on social media. “A strong, coordinated US response is essential to safeguard fair treatment of US companies and maintain strategic balance against China’s growing economic influence in the sector.”
Republican Representative Darrell Issa also mentioned Coupang as one of the US tech firms subject to “a campaign of aggression” from the South Korean government.
“Specifically, this is being done by new digital trade laws that American companies will tell you simultaneously favour Korean and Chinese businesses and strangle their ability to operate in Korea,” he said in a column published on Monday by the Daily Caller, a US right-wing news website.

Real America’s Voice, a US-based right-wing streaming channel, reported the same day that South Korean President Lee Jae-myung issued a directive to bankrupt Coupang.
The channel also shared Issa’s supposition that South Korea’s regulation of Coupang and other US tech firms led to the cancellation of last Thursday’s closed-door meeting of the Korea-US Free Trade Agreement Joint Committee, despite the South Korean government’s explanation that the event was postponed because both sides needed more time to discuss the details of the agenda.
According to the US Senate’s lobbying disclosures, Coupang has spent at least US$10 million in the US since August 2021. The company also donated US$1 million to Trump’s second inauguration ceremony in January.
Given the company’s aggressive recruitment of lobbyists in Washington, concerns are mounting that the US may intensify its pressure on South Korea to stop the investigation into the company.
In response, the presidential office ordered the foreign minister and national security officials to attend an unusual ministerial meeting on Christmas Day to discuss countermeasures against Coupang’s data breach.
The Foreign Affairs and Unification Committee will also join the National Assembly’s joint hearing scheduled for December 30 and 31 to examine whether the Coupang case will affect diplomatic relations with the US.
Coupang on Thursday said the customer information leaked ⁠from the company has been deleted by ‍the suspect.
This story was first published by The Korea Times

data.title: Thousands stuck for hours at Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge on Christmas Day
data.description:
Tens of thousands of Hongkongers were stuck for hours at the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge on Christmas Day, as residents streamed out of the city’s border checkpoints for the holiday.
Police and rail giant the MTR Corporation also warned on Thursday morning that Shenzhen Bay, Lo Wu and Lok Ma Chau spur line control points were crowded and that travellers should expect a longer time to cross the border.
The wave of outbound travel started on Christmas Eve on Wednesday, when the net outflow of people from the city through all checkpoints jumped almost 44 per cent to 298,085 compared with the same day last year, according to official statistics.
This year’s Christmas Day falls on Thursday and Boxing Day on Friday, forming a four-day long weekend.
A Post reporter at the Hong Kong port of the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge observed at around lunchtime that the departure hall was packed with tens of thousands of tourists and visitors, who waited at least 1½ hours in lines just to get on the border-crossing Gold Bus.
At the mainland China side of Luohu, crowds packed the entire open space off the shopping centre connected to the checkpoint.
Many revellers were seen putting on the signature red and white Christmas hats.
At the West Kowloon terminus of the Hong Kong section of the Guangzhou-Shenzhen-Hong Kong Express Rail Link, travellers – many with children – filled the concourse.
Many were travelling to destinations besides Shenzhen, such as Foshan, Zhanjiang and Jiangxi.
A Hong Kong resident in his thirties, surnamed Hon, was travelling with his wife, son and daughter, and all of them were in a celebratory mood and putting on Christmas hats.
“Spending for the whole family is more economical in Shenzhen, we plan to spend less than HK$10,000 [US$1,286] for several days,” Hon said.
Another traveller, called Ms Tam, was heading to Zhanjiang with several friends and budgeted about 3,000 to 4,000 yuan (HK$3,300 to HK$4,400) per person for a four-day holiday.
“It is because spending there is value for money,” she said.
On Christmas Eve, the government said it had coordinated with various local and cross-border transport operators to boost their services during the long-weekend to accommodate the public’s travelling needs.
These included shuttle buses on the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge, cross-border coaches and the MTR.

data.title: Indonesians see red after Bonnie Blue uses flag to ‘wipe the floor’
data.description:
Controversial British adult film star Bonnie Blue has again earned the wrath of Indonesia after a video of her insulting the Southeast Asian nation’s flag went viral on social media.
In a clip filmed in front of Jakarta’s embassy in London, Blue, who was deported from Bali earlier this month, is seen holding the red-and-white flag surrounded by a group of masked men.
“Yes, I got arrested in Bali, and so for my last time, I came to the embassy so they could watch in person,” said the 26-year-old, whose real name is Tia Billinger.
As the crowd next to her cheered and clapped at the provocative stunt, Blue pinned the flag to the back of her dress, causing it to drag on the ground as she walked.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Francesco Angeloni (@canggulosophy)



“People said I’ve disrespected Bali culture. Instead, I’m going to use this to wipe the floor,” she said.
The adult content creator added that she visited the embassy to “pay my £8.55”, an apparent reference to the fine imposed on her for violating the Indonesian holiday island’s traffic law.
Indonesia, which has banned Blue for 10 years, lodged a formal complaint with the UK’s foreign ministry and police over Tuesday’s incident, the Jakarta Globe reported.
“The red and white flag is a symbol of sovereignty, honour and national identity that must be respected by everyone, wherever they are,” Indonesia’s foreign ministry said on Wednesday.
Some Indonesian social media users also poured scorn on Blue’s “cheap” stunt, urging others to mass report her online accounts in a bid to get them suspended.
A commenter said Blue should raise her grievances with the government and not disrespect the flag, which is “a symbol of our struggle and pride”.

Blue’s run-in with Indonesia began on December 4, when police arrested her alongside more than a dozen men on suspicion of making pornographic content at a studio in Bali.
Police, however, did not press charges as the material was for “private documentation and not for public distribution”.
A Bali court later ordered Blue to leave the country and fined her US$12 for traffic violations.
The Muslim-majority country strictly forbids the production of pornographic material, which carries penalties of up to 12 years in prison and a fine of US$360,000.
After arriving in the UK about two weeks ago, Blue also bragged about how she got off lightly.
“I’m rich and have good lawyers – did you really think I’d face jail time?” she said, according to Us Weekly.

data.title: Fujian enters service, J-10C’s combat action: 10 China military developments in 2025
data.description:
Catch up on some of SCMP’s biggest China military stories of the year. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. China’s most advanced aircraft carrier, the Fujian, enters service
China’s third aircraft carrier, the Fujian, entered into service on Wednesday at a grand ceremony in Sanya, Hainan province, according to state media reports on Friday.
2. How did China’s J-10C match up to French Rafale in India-Pakistan aerial clash?
Reports that France’s advanced Rafale fighter jet came off second best against the China-made J-10C Vigorous Dragon in a dogfight between the Indian and Pakistan air forces on May 7 attracted global attention.
3. Will the JL-1 missile be China’s final word on airborne nuclear deterrence?

The debut of the Jinglei-1 (JL-1) long-range missile means China has a complete air, sea and land nuclear deterrent, one that will improve with the next generation of strategic bombers, according to analysts.
4. Chinese military’s new underwater unmanned systems are ‘disruptive’: journal
A Chinese military journal has described the People’s Liberation Army’s latest underwater unmanned systems as “disruptive” and highlighted their intelligent capabilities.
5. China’s jumbo Sichuan 076 amphibious assault ship completes first sea trials
China’s next-generation “drone-carrier” amphibious assault ship, the Sichuan, completed its first sea trials on Sunday afternoon, state broadcaster CCTV said.
6. AI, lasers and chips: the science and tech of China’s military parade

China’s military parade on September 3 – featuring the latest AI-powered uncrewed vehicles, laser weapons and missiles – signalled an arms race fuelled by scientific and technological advances.
7. Beyond the parade, what China’s new stealth drones mean for future air combat
Among the many pieces of advanced military hardware showcased during the Victory Day parade in Beijing on September 3 were at least four unnamed drones, at least one of which was a “loyal wingman”, a stealth attack drone supposedly capable of coordinated aerial warfare alongside crewed jets, according to state broadcaster CCTV.
8. Chinese weapons giant develops anti-drone barrage system to beat wartime swarms
China has unveiled the world’s first close-in anti-drone barrage weapon system, a groundbreaking innovation against drone swarms and high-speed missiles, according to its developer.
9. Chinese military lab creates mosquito-sized microdrone for covert operations
A robotics laboratory at the National University of Defence Technology (NUDT) in central China’s Hunan province has developed a mosquito-sized drone for covert military operations.
10. Chinese scientists build first jet fuel-powered engine for Mach 16 flight
The world’s first oblique detonation engine (ODE) powered by standard aviation kerosene has been successfully tested by Chinese scientists, marking a potential game-changer in hypersonic propulsion that could redefine the limits of air and space travel.

data.title: Chinese SUV maker Jetour bets on localised product offerings to hasten go-global drive
data.description:
Chinese sports utility vehicle maker Jetour, with an eye on becoming a global SUV powerhouse, plans to accelerate its overseas expansion in the Middle East with products tailored to local motorists.
Already present in more than 100 countries and regions after seven years in operation, Jetour said at a recent fan gathering in Abu Dhabi that it was banking on local research and development (R&D) efforts to design and build world-class SUVs for countries in the Middle East.
“We are listening closely to user feedback through product experience,” Jetour said in a written reply to the Post. “We will deliver more than just cars [in overseas markets]”, adding that the company would bring premium services, as well as supply chains and lifestyle campaigns tailored for each market.
Jetour, which builds both petrol-powered and hybrid SUVs, had hosted a fan gathering on Saturday at the Liwa International Festival – an annual winter event in Abu Dhabi that attracts about 600,000 visitors – to support its business in the Middle East, where the firm offers market-specific features such as full-vehicle heat-insulated glass.
It was part of Jetour’s focus to bring motorists in the region more lifestyle experiences through its smart cockpits and artificial intelligence-assisted driving systems, backed by new battery technologies and automotive chips.
Jetour’s participation at the event underscored the company’s efforts to burnish its credentials as a major supplier of off-road vehicles to customers in the region. According to the firm, its vehicles developed for the Middle East also featured side-window sunshades, dual sun visors and enhanced cooling performance.
In November, Jetour’s G700 hybrid SUV made its debut in Dubai. Prices for this model start at US$48,740 in the United Arab Emirates. It was expected to be available next month in Saudi Arabia.

A subsidiary of state-owned Chery Holding Group, Jetour has been pushing a go-global programme distinct from what other major Chinese carmakers have been doing.
Since it was founded in 2018, Jetour has focused on establishing 10 R&D centres along with 19 production bases around the globe. It also distributed its SUVs via more than 2,000 outlets.
“At present, few Chinese carmakers have effectively localised their production bases and R&D facilities in their major target markets abroad,” said Gao Shen, an independent analyst in Shanghai. “Most of them are exporting vehicles developed for Chinese drivers and passengers to overseas markets, hoping their intelligent features and high-performance batteries can convince local customers.”
As of November, Jetour delivered nearly 2.1 million SUVs to buyers. More than a quarter of those were completed in markets outside mainland China.
In 2024, the company reported total sales of 568,000 vehicles, up 80 per cent from the previous year.
Chinese carmakers, led by electric vehicle giant BYD, currently serve as the vanguard of electrification and digitalisation, thanks to government support and local consumers’ willingness to embrace innovation, according to analysts.
These carmakers normally fine-tune their models in foreign markets to bolster overseas shipments, while complying with local rules and regulations.
The average net per-vehicle ­margin – the gap between the ­selling price and production costs such as raw materials, labour and logistics – stood at about 5,000 yuan (US$711) among Chinese carmakers, according to Nick Lai, head of auto research in Asia-Pacific at JPMorgan.
But the margin could jump fourfold to 20,000 yuan if the cars were sold in overseas markets, where they could go for higher prices, Lai told the Post in October.

data.title: Bronze Age cemetery offers up clues to how ancient Chinese brewed red rice wine
data.description:
Pottery vessels containing traces of alcohol from a Bronze Age cemetery in northwestern China indicate that people at the time consumed beverages made using a fermentation starter of rice and a red mould.
Archaeologists said the drink, similar to red rice wine, played a key role in their mortuary rituals, and its presence at the Mogou cemetery suggested a tradition that probably served to strengthen the bond between the living and the dead.
Researchers from Northwest University in Xian, the Gansu Provincial Institute of Cultural Relics and Archaeology, and Stanford University in California published their findings in the peer-reviewed Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports in October.
The site, which was in use for 600 years between 1700 and 1100 BC, is in the northwestern province of Gansu and on the northeastern margin of the Tibetan Plateau. The cemetery, where 5,000 people were buried, was excavated between 2008 and 2012.
It is the largest cemetery in the upper Tao River valley, the second largest tributary in the upper reaches of the Yellow River valley. It is also a significant step along a key route linking Central Asia and Central China and a prime location for cultural exchanges over the centuries.
The researchers described an alcohol fermentation method that used a starter – known in Chinese as qu – made up of cereals, moulds and yeasts.
While malt-based fermentation relies on the enzymes produced during cereal germination and added yeast to produce alcohol, when a starter is used it generates the yeast and enzymes itself from the microbes it contains.

The brewing process involves mixing the starter with cooked starchy ingredients – such as millet, rice, wheat or barley – to produce sugars and alcohol simultaneously, a process similar to that used today to produce rice wines such as sake.
To try to understand the role alcoholic drinks played in mortuary rituals, the team analysed residue samples from 42 pottery vessels, most of them crafted from fine clay, that were found in four tombs.
They said 20 vessels appeared to have been used for holding fermented drinks or food. The residues found in them contained fermentation-related moulds and yeast cells – suggesting they were used to make alcohol.
“This frequent co-occurrence of rice starch grains with Monascus moulds and yeast cells aligns with the long-standing tradition of red rice alcohol production in China during the Neolithic and early Bronze Age, as previously documented,” the researchers wrote.
“These findings suggest that rice at Mogou was most likely used for brewing alcohol, involving a qu starter composed of Monascus mould and rice as the fermenting agent.”
Monascus, which produces a bright red colour, has long been used as a fermentation starter, food pigment and traditional Chinese medicine ingredient.
It is typically found in the red rice qu starter used to brew traditional rice-based red beer, often called red rice alcohol or red alcohol, the paper added.
This Bronze Age brewing tradition, which began in eastern China during the Neolithic period and later expanded westward, continued to play a central role in mortuary rituals at Mogou for more than six centuries, according to the study.
“The presence of this beverage in mortuary contexts at Mogou aligns with mortuary practices from earlier Neolithic cultures, suggesting that it was closely associated with ceremonial activities, particularly in ancestral worship and funerary rituals.
“This tradition likely served to strengthen the connection between the living and the dead, acting as a medium for preserving a collective memory of ancestors among community descendants,” they wrote.

data.title: China university offers US$11,000 ‘micro’ overseas study to help students pass failed courses
data.description:
A privately run college in China has come under fire for asking students who failed exams to pay as much as 79,800 yuan (US$11,000) to join a nonexistent “micro study abroad” programme to have their fail record removed.
Xian Technology and Business College, financed by Beijing Northern Investment Group, is a little-known institute in Xian, Shaanxi province, northwestern China.
In mid-December, some parents complained to the news outlet Benliu News that teachers at the school coerced them into paying a large sum to have their children's exam failures corrected.
“They intentionally let my kid fail in the test. A teacher called me, saying that since my kid has failed in four tests, we have to drop out of school or retake the year,” a parent told the media.

“Then the teacher suggested a new solution which is to pay 79,800 yuan to join a ‘micro study abroad’ programme. Students will have their test failure records deleted if they join this programme,” said the parent.
Students who take this option would also be entitled to “priority treatment” when choosing dormitories and competing for scholarships, the teacher hinted, according to the parent whose name was not released in the report.
The “micro study abroad” programme would, in reality, not be held, the teacher told the parent.
“The teacher told me that students applying for this programme do not need to go abroad and there are no study activities either,” the parent said.

After the parent transferred the money to an account provided by the teacher, the receiver turned out to be a hi-tech company in Yili, Xinjiang Uygur autonomous region.
The school did not provide an invoice for this payment.
The incident triggered outrage online after it was viewed 10 million times on social media.
“It is beyond my comprehension. How could this be traded? The school has become a business,” one internet user said.
Another person said: “The authorities should investigate thoroughly and punish concerning individuals seriously.”
The news outlet gmw.cn slammed the Xian college in an editorial on December 15.

“When paying 79,800 yuan can ensure you pass the test, scores have thus become tradeable commodities and education fairness has become a privileged service which is sold at a clearly marked price,” the article said.
It also pointed out that if the school does not carry out the so-called micro study abroad programme and charges fees, it might have committed fraud.
Amid immense public pressure, the Shaanxi Education Department said on December 16 that an investigation had been launched.
In China, most universities and colleges are government-funded.
There are only about 800 privately run tertiary education institutions across the country, very few of which are highly regarded by the public.

data.title: ‘Holding on to hope’: Hong Kong fire survivors make New Year wish for a home
data.description:
Retired Hong Kong civil servant Ng Kook-yiu takes a shuttle bus every day from his transitional housing flat to the fire-ravaged Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po.
“I long to see my home, where I spent four decades of my life. It breaks my heart to see it each time, but it is still my home,” the 76-year-old said.
Ng lived alone in the housing estate before the city’s deadliest blaze in decades engulfed seven of its eight buildings on November 26, killing at least 161 people.
Like other residents who were offered temporary accommodation, Ng moved into a flat of just more than 100 sq ft at the Good House transitional housing site after the inferno.
He said he had since travelled back to the estate daily to check on it and to collect free items, including daily necessities and clothes distributed by charities to affected residents.
He also receives free lunches at a dim sum shop in the nearby Kwong Fuk Commercial Centre during his trips home. The eatery offers complimentary meals to Wang Fuk Court residents in the aftermath of the tragedy.
Ng said it would be a sombre Christmas in his temporary flat, but he remained hopeful. His wish for the New Year, he added, was for a long-term resettlement option.
“I hope things will be different and turn for the better in the new year,” he said.
Displaced residents of the Tai Po estate continue to struggle to resume normal life. Many long to return or be resettled, as they prepare to spend their first Christmas and New Year’s Day in temporary flats since the blaze destroyed their homes.
Authorities are now gathering residents’ views on various resettlement options, which include purchasing property rights from homeowners, giving displaced residents priority for subsidised Home Ownership Scheme flats, constructing new housing in Tai Po, and allowing eligible residents to move into public rental housing.
The Health Bureau said on Thursday that of the 79 patients treated in public hospitals after the fire, 65 had recovered and been discharged, while the remaining 14 were in stable condition as of Tuesday.
The government’s mental health support hotline had received more than 8,300 calls as of Tuesday, about 660 of them related to the fire.
Retiree David Wong, 73, is now staying at the Po Leung Kuk Lee Shau Kee Youth Oasis in Yuen Long.
Wong, who had lived in Wang Fuk Court with his son for more than 10 years, said he was slowly adapting to life in the youth hostel.
“In Tai Po, it was my own place. In the youth hostel, it is not,” he said. “If you were playing something out loud here, you can’t have the volume too loud. You have to adapt to the new environment.
“Back home, I could turn my television up and there was no issue at all.”

Wong’s son, whose job takes him to different parts of the city, now splits his time between staying with his father and others, unable to endure the long commute to and from Yuen Long.
“There is not much that my son can do. When he commutes to and from work, he will end up wasting between one and two hours, so it is really tough,” Wong said.
He added that he had no plans to celebrate Christmas or the New Year, citing the emotional toll of the fire, the need to apply for help schemes and uncertainty over his long-term resettlement.
“I see that most of the Wang Fuk Court residents that are staying in the youth hostel aren’t in any mood to celebrate holidays like [last Sunday’s] winter solstice,” he said.
“They are more concerned about having a roof over their head and a meal to fill their stomach; they are not thinking of anything else at the moment.”
Other homesick residents, such as Raymond Cheung and his wife, Pinky Cheung, both around 70 and retired, also continue to make regular trips back to the neighbourhood, expressing hope of one day returning there.
The couple returned on Tuesday from a friend’s home where they had been staying since the fire. They previously lived in Wang Yan House and were among the first batch of homeowners in the estate.
“The home is the culmination of my hard work, and I bought it with my hard-earned money,” the wife said.
“If the government tells us now that we can go back to Wang Fuk Court, I will definitely go back and stay there.”

data.title: Philippine court upholds murder verdict in drug war case: what does it mean for Duterte?
data.description:
The Philippine Supreme Court has upheld the murder conviction of three policemen who killed a teenage boy at the height of former president Rodrigo Duterte’s war on drugs in 2017, a watershed moment that legal analysts say affirms the country’s justice system.
In a decision made public on Monday, the court found Arnel Oares, Jeremias Pereda and Jerwin Cruz guilty of murdering 17-year-old high school student Kian delos Santos while conducting an anti-drug operation in the city of Caloocan in Metro Manila.
They were each sentenced to 40 years’ jail and would have to pay the teen’s family a total of 275,000 pesos (US$4,700).
The decision affirmed earlier rulings by a regional trial court and the Court of Appeals that had convicted the policemen without eligibility for parole, despite multiple appeals from the defendants.
The policemen were first found guilty in 2018, a year after delos Santos’ death.

Opposition senator Risa Hontiveros, who led Senate investigations into delos Santos’ murder in 2017, lauded the ruling.
“The Supreme Court’s decision is clear: Kian delos Santos had been helplessly killed. There was no encounter, and he did not fight back. The three officers’ alibis have no truth to them,” she said on Tuesday.
The decision was “an inspiring development for human rights advocates, lawyers and civic groups, and other families of tokhang victims,” she said, using the colloquial term for the drug war.
Delos Santos’ case proved that the killings during the drug war were systemic, she said. “Accountability should also be demanded from officials who attempted to cover up the incident, and even made up lies about Kian and his family to justify his death.”
This is the first of a growing list of cases involving minors killed during Duterte’s war on drugs. Although police have admitted to killing over 6,000 throughout the drug war from 2016 to 2022, human rights watchdogs estimate this number to be much higher, at about 30,000.
Shot while kneeling
Analysts say the court’s decision was significant as it affirmed the correct application of self-defence, which the police had used to justify their killing of delos Santos.
Earlier police reports stated that the teen had attempted to flee when the officers approached him, while also drawing a firearm, prompting Oares to fire back in self-defence. Delos Santos was later found with a pistol, cartridges and two sachets of methamphetamine.
However, surveillance footage and eyewitness accounts refuted the claims. Videos showed that after having beaten delos Santos and accused him of possessing illegal drugs, the police dragged him into a dark alleyway, where he was shot.
An autopsy showed delos Santos was likely kneeling on the ground when he was shot, consistent with the regional court’s findings in 2018. The Court of Appeals also affirmed this ruling.
This was in line with witnesses saying they heard delos Santos pleading for his life, saying he had an exam at school the next day.

The sequence of events established treachery, an important element in ascertaining murder. According to the Supreme Court, treachery “exists when the victim has no chance to defend themselves, and the attacker deliberately chooses the means or method of attack”.
Ross Tugade, an international legal scholar and assistant counsel to the International Criminal Court (ICC), told This Week in Asia that the Philippine court’s decision was significant as delos Santos’ case “has gripped our collective sense of the pursuit of justice in the drug war”.
“Kian’s case is what we could call in international criminal investigations as ‘emblematic cases’ or those that demonstrate the pattern and methods of mass atrocity crimes,” he said.
“As Kian’s killing took place during the peak of the drug war, the circumstances surrounding it revealed the workings of the state policy during Duterte’s time – namely arbitrary killing, ‘one time big time’ operations with quotas, false narratives on innocent people, and the planting of evidence, among others.”
The court further dismissed Oares’ argument that he shot delos Santos while performing his duties as a police officer, adding that “this defence applies only when the harm is an unavoidable result of a lawful police action”.
“The killing of a minor could not be considered standard in this operation”, it said, adding that the “performance of duties does not include murder”.
‘Very encouraging’
Despite the death arising from a state-sanctioned operation, the ruling was consistent with criminal law and the Supreme Court’s adherence to its principles, Darwin Angeles, a lecturer at the University of the Philippines’ College of Law, said, calling it “very encouraging for similarly situated victims”.
The court also rejected officers’ defence that the teen’s death had been a regular consequence of police operations. In the past, the former president had admitted that he had encouraged officers to provoke suspects into fighting back so they could be killed.
“Duterte encouraged the use by police officers of lethal force on the premise that they can exculpate themselves later on with a claim of self-defence. This case shows that Duterte, despite being a former prosecutor, was mistaken,” Angeles said.
However, analysts said that the Supreme Court’s decision, although significant, might only marginally affect Duterte’s ICC case.


The former president remains detained at The Hague and awaits trial for charges of crimes against humanity for the killings committed during his administration’s bloody drug war.
“The [court’s] ruling in Kian’s case can help the prosecution’s case albeit marginally,” Angeles said. So while evidence of wrongdoing by police officers can be helpful, the defence can argue that delos Santos’ case was isolated.
However, the decision may further prove the necessity of the ICC in exercising its jurisdiction over Duterte’s case.
One conviction over “thousands of questionable killings” and the length of time needed to “fully secure accountability” showed that the country’s judicial system might not be adequate, Angeles said.
Tugade said that the Supreme Court’s decision had affirmed one case of murder, which could not be contextualised within a larger pattern of crimes against humanity. In addition, those convicted were “foot soldiers”, not high-profile officials who had shaped the war on drugs.
“This will likely not trigger issues of admissibility because the ICC is interested in the pattern of crimes against humanity and the perpetrator structure, especially those most responsible for the crime,” Tugade said.
